dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 for a 73 y/o female with a reported seizure and altered mental status. [CITY], urgent.
Hi, I'm Tess, an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
She started a seizure about 45 minutes ago. She was lying on the couch and not responding.
Are you able to open your eyes for me and look towards your surroundings?
(Gazes slightly to the left; one eye opens minimally)
Can you say "yes" or shake your head for me?
No verbal or gross motor response observed. We notice mild twitching of the left cheek.
Airway is patent and self-maintained. Dentures have been removed and bagged for protection.
Breathing frequency is 10 breaths per minute, shallow with minimal use of accessory muscles. No chest asymmetry seen.
Radial pulse 80 beats per minute, irregular, and weak. Skin is pale, warm, and dry.
Blood pressure is currently 151/81 mmHg.
Respiration remains at 10 breaths per minute and shallow, no asymmetry.
SpO₂ is 76% on room air. We are placing you on 10 LPM O2 via non-rebreather now.
Glucose is not detectable, but not critical at this time. Let me recheck later.
Let's start the EKG now. I'll apply electrodes and monitor for any arrhythmias.
I'll recheck her pulse while the monitor is on. Her baseline heart rate is 80 beats per minute and irregular.
Skin is dry, pale, and without rashes or bruising. Bystanders confirm she's never had any bite marks.
Can you tell me about her known allergies or prescription medications?
Schizoaffective disorder and a history of seizures. On medications: Flexeril, Protonix, Lasix, Digoxin, and Toprol.
What symptoms or sensations did she describe before the episode began today?
She said the sun was bothering her. No known vomiting or injuries today before the seizure.
She flew in from [STATE] earlier today. No signs of illness other than mental health symptom flare-ups.
Has she eaten or drank anything today before the call?
She had some water after arriving. She likely hadn't eaten yet today.
On a 0 to 10 scale, how would you rate her pain right now if possible?
She's unresponsive and appears in no pain-score would be 0.
We are administering midazolam (Versed) intranasally now to manage seizure activity.
Attaching you to a cardiac monitor and starting the EKG. This helps detect any concerning rhythms.
Based on her age, mental status, and prior non-epileptic seizures, we're applying the Seizure Protocol (Protocol 3-12).
After Versed administration, vitals show increased heart rate of 159 with BP of 191/122. EKG shows atrial fibrillation with RVR and PVCs.
Respiration rate increased to 20 with some labored breaths. SpO₂ improved to 99%. We can reduce O2 to 4 LPM via nasal cannula.
Her heart rate remains 138 with stable BP at 127/73.
Respiration rate is 26 now with improvement in depth. SpO₂ continues at 99%.
SpO₂ is 99%. I'll continue monitoring and O₂ may remain at 4 LPM.
EKG still shows atrial fibrillation with RVR and PVCs, but not STEMI-no acute infarct. Some readings showed bigeminy, a premature beat pattern.
First IV placed in her left arm. It didn't hold, so we placed another in the same area. Saline lock now active.
She showed a STEMI-reading EKG earlier, but manual review didn't confirm acute anterior infarct. Will reassess if changes occur.
We gave 500 ml of normal saline IV. Heart rate dropped to 140 but remains irregular.
I'll monitor vitals again soon for consistent tracking of her condition.
BP is now 117/72, heart rate at 129 with 4 LPM O2.
Respiration is 22 breaths per minute, normal in depth and pattern.
Latest EKG reassesses AFib with RVR and occasional bigeminy-no new rhythms or infarct signs.
Which hospital would you prefer for her treatment?
[CITY] is the usual one. They handle her condition.
We'll lift her onto the stretcher now. We need your help safely turning her over.
Of course, we can assist.
She's on the stretcher and secured using five-point restraints. All settings confirmed.
We noticed sonorous breathing. Inserted a 26Fr nasopharyngeal airway in the right nostril.
Sonorous sounds cleared. Her breathing is now clear at 22 breaths per minute with SpO₂ at 95%.
Transport to [CITY] emergency with continuous ECG monitoring. En route, she's stable but requires close observation.
Airway is secured with NPA. Administering fluids continuously, and monitoring for any change in rhythm.
We now have all subjective history. Her prior episodes were non-epileptic and observation-focused.
She is ready for transport. Secured, covered, and restraints are secure. Hospital has been informed.
Her responsiveness remained unchanged during securement. GCS is maintained at 6.
She had respiratory rate at 10, 20, and now 22 at different stages-no consistent rhythm found.
She is being positioned in semi-Fowler's to improve breathing compliance.
